articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — The colored Philadelphian in literature
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Sep. 12, 1914. Survey of Black Philadelphia in books: Watson's Annals, Walker's Appeal (1830) on Bishop Allen, Absalom Jones's 1799 anti-deportation protest, Russell Parrott (1814) and Saunders (1818) printed addresses, Bishop Allen's autobiography, the first colored church hymn book (Richard Allen with Chapman, 1796 — one copy in the J. P. Morgan collection, another owned by Dr. Henry M. Minton), Rev. William Douglass's 'Annals of St. Thomas' built from the Free African Society records, Martin R. Delany's 'Destiny of the Colored Race,' Rev. Noah Cannon's 'Rock of Ages,' William Still's and Smedley's Underground Railroad books (the latter partly edited by Robert Purvis), Frances E. W. Harper's works, and Trotter's 'Music and Some Musical People.'

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Hezekiah Grice and the Convention of 1830
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 29, 1914. Full account of the first national colored convention, Bethel Church, September 1830: conceived by Hezekiah Grice of Baltimore (associate of Lundy and Garrison), who won Bishop Richard Allen's cooperation two years before Allen's death. Delegates included Christopher Rush, Peter Williams, Philip A. Bell, William Hamilton, Peter Vogelsang and Samuel Cornish of New York; Austin Steward of Rochester; Watkins of Baltimore (father of Frances E. W. Harper); Lewis Woodson and John Peck of Pittsburgh; William Jennings of Trenton; Abram D. Shadd of Delaware; J. W. C. Pennington; and from Philadelphia James Forten, William Whipper, Bishop Morris Brown, Cyrus Black, Junius C. Morel, Frederick A. Hinton, Belfast Burton, Robert Douglass Sr., Jonathan Tudas, LeCount, John Bowers and Robert C. Gordon. Grice emigrated to Haiti in 1831; his daughter married Rev. William Douglass.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Early journalists and the Daily Advocate
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 11, 1914. On the apprenticeships of Chris. J. Perry (Sunday Mirror), Samuel B. Hart and Charles Howard Johnson; the Daily Advocate of 1877, its advertisers (James LeCount, undertaker; Thomas H. Boling, grocer) and its Feb. 2, 1877 Grey Invincibles entertainment; dance master James Augustus; and James McCrummell's paper 'The Elevator,' published over sixty years earlier — a potent factor in the people's betterment.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — The women who broke the traces
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 10, 1914. Bolivar's women's history: Phillis Wheatley and Deborah Gannett; the women who nursed under Absalom Jones, Richard Allen and William Gray in the 1793 yellow fever; the unnamed Black woman teacher in the Quaker school of c.1802; Grace Douglass in the founding of First African Presbyterian; Mary Lewton, founder-spirit of Union A.M.E., author of a tract on 'Sanctification,' whose home sheltered ~2,000 fugitives ending with Dangerfield in 1859; Sarah M. Douglass, sixty-year teacher, first secretary of the Female Anti-Slavery Society, correspondent of Fredrika Bremer and the Grimké sisters, contributor to The Liberator; Jarena Lee, authorized by Bishop Allen; Hester Reckless, teacher and Underground Railroad worker trusted by Lucretia Mott; Ann Appo, first of the race to play a church organ (1834); Elizabeth Kenton and Mary Eustis, 'Creation' soloists 1840-41; Elizabeth Taylor Greenfield; Frances E. W. Harper; Fanny Jackson-Coppin; Harriet Tubman; Grace Mapps, first college graduate; Charlotte Forten Grimké.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Col. John W. Forney and The Press
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Dec. 7, 1912. On the friendship of Col. John W. Forney's Philadelphia Press: T. Morris Chester's letters from Russia (first colored special writer on a white daily), Eleanor J. Ketchum, John S. Durham and Florence Lewis ('Alice Irving') on its staff; its open columns to William Still, Jacob C. White Jr., Octavius V. Catto, William D. Forten, Robert Purvis, Parker T. Smith and St. George R. Taylor; the Social, Civil and Statistical Association lecture course of 1867-68 (over $7,000 for freedmen's schools); Forney's praise of Robert Purvis and Rev. Stephen Smith; his son commanded the marines during the Catto riots.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Necrology of 1913
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Dec. 27, 1913. The year's death roll (~90 names with dates), led by Joanna Dorsey, dead past her 97th milestone, a Bethel member who received religious instruction under Bishop Richard Allen; also Fanny M. Jackson Coppin (Jan. 21, 1913), Andrew J. Jones (June 30), John W. Kelsey, Charles Purnell, John Harkless and others.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — The campaign of 1872, the first colored vote
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Nov. 30, 1912. The 1872 presidential campaign — the first in which Philadelphia's colored men voted, in the shadow of Catto's 1871 murder. Platform orators included William D. Forten, Capt. James A. Junior, Major John W. Simpson, Francis P. Main and Rev. James Underdue; Louis N. Bedford marshaled Frankford, Captain Ransom Germantown; Stephen B. Gipson emerged as a new campaign speaker.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Turner's 'The Negro in Pennsylvania' reviewed
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Feb. 14, 1914. Review of Edward Raymond Turner's book: the colonization fight, Absalom Jones's sermon against deportation at St. Thomas' (so powerful a U.S. Senate committee of inquiry formed under Senator Rutledge of South Carolina), the War of 1812 service under Decatur, Porter and Jackson, the first secular convention of colored people in the United States (Philadelphia, 1817, with James Forten, Absalom Jones and Richard Allen), and the St. Mary Street school of 1823, the first under the municipality — while faulting Turner for omitting fifty years of school taxes paid by Black citizens without public school returns.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — A Christian Recorder of August 1865
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Mar. 8, 1913. Close reading of the Christian Recorder of Aug. 12, 1865 (Rev. Elisha Weaver, editor; correspondents B. T. Tanner, A. L. Stanford, Samuel Watts, T. M. D. Ward): letters from the 54th Massachusetts and 6th U.S.C.T.; the death (July 27, 1865) and biography of Rev. Jeremiah Asher, Shiloh's pastor and 6th U.S.C.T. chaplain; a monument subscription signed by Henry Highland Garnet, Stephen Smith, Bishops Clinton, Payne and Nazrey; and Bolivar's discovery of the A.M.E. Church Magazines of 1841-45.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — The old Eighth Ward
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Sep. 19, 1914. Historical geography of the Eighth Ward: Robert Bogle, father of the catering business, immortalized in verse by Nicholas Biddle; the colored inventor of ice cream named Jackson near the Philadelphia Saving Fund; musician William Appo and Edward Roland (cornetist of Frank Johnson's band, later Excelsior Band leader); the Benezet school of 1770, the city's oldest; the old family seats (Newmans, Mintons, Roberts, Baptists, Alexanders); John Moore the sailmaker; residences of Henry Jones, Henry Minton, William Still and William Warrick on Twelfth Street; St. Thomas' removal to Twelfth near Walnut; and Frémont's 1856 nomination at Musical Fund Hall.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Camp William Penn and the black soldiery
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 3, 1914. On Gen. Louis Wagner and Philadelphia's 16,000 Black Civil War soldiers: the July 1863 emergency enrollment of ninety young men (depleting the Institute for Colored Youth of teachers Jacob C. White Jr. and Octavius V. Catto and many scholars); survivors Raymond J. Burr and Henry Boyer Jr.; William Still as sutler at Camps Cadwalader and William Penn; the Women's Union raising money for soldiers' comforts (Caroline LeCount, Amelia Mills among survivors); Congressional medal winners Thomas Hawkins and Navy gunner John Lawson at Mobile Bay; and ~4,000 men of color in the Revolution, including Colonel Israel Shreve's mixed command.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — John Brown remembered
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Dec. 13, 1913. On John Brown's execution (Dec. 2, 1859) and Philadelphia's response: the sympathy meeting at National Hall on execution day sponsored by Rev. Dr. Furness, George H. Earle and Edward T. Hooper; the fearless approval of Stephen Smith, Robert Purvis, Isaiah C. Wear, John C. Bowers and colleagues; Jessie Ewing Glasgow — a Philadelphia student at a Scottish university — whose 1861 published review of Brown is now a rarity of racial literature; and the Dangerfield rescue, the last fugitive slave case in the city's courts.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — Rev. Jeremiah Asher and Shiloh's story
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Sep. 28, 1912. On Rev. Jeremiah Asher's 1862 history of Shiloh Baptist Church (the city's third Baptist church, originally on Clifton Street below South): Asher, a Connecticut native and grandson of a Revolutionary soldier, rescued the church from a precarious state, raised funds in England, and opened a day school within its walls sixty-five years ago. Records the official staff of 1861: deacons Joseph Watson, Spencer Mitchell, John S. Renney and W. F. Keeling; trustees Robert Ruffin, Edwin James, James M. Auter, Thomas W. Evans and Addison W. Lively; treasurer James M. Auter; church clerk John Brown. Franklin Guinn later continued the church's story.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points — The musical pioneers: Frank Johnson's band
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Apr. 1913. Music retrospective: Matt Black's band formed 1818; John Cromwell's eight singing classes before and after 1820 (pupils included Morris Brown Jr. and Robert Johnson); Frank Johnson's band formed in the 1820s, which went to England in 1838 and played at Windsor Castle before Queen Victoria — Johnson personally complimented and presented a gold-tipped bugle. Brings the line forward through William H. Cole's chamber music at an American Negro Historical Society lecture, the Peoples Chorus 'Elijah,' Coleridge-Taylor and Will Marion Cook.

events.name: First National Colored Convention, Bethel Church
events.description: The first national convention of colored people, conceived by Hezekiah Grice and hosted by Bishop Richard Allen; trend was general betterment along with the battle against slavery (recalled in Pencil Pusher Points, Aug. 29, 1914).

events.name: First secular convention of colored people (anti-colonization)
events.description: The first secular convention ever held by colored people in the United States, fighting the colonization/deportation scheme after the War of 1812 (per Bolivar's review of Turner, Feb. 14, 1914).

events.name: John Brown sympathy meeting, National Hall
events.description: Meeting of sympathy held on the day of John Brown's execution; colored Philadelphians gave fearless outspoken approval (recalled Dec. 13, 1913).

events.name: Shiloh Baptist Church official staff of 1861
events.description: Church officers recorded in Asher's history (recalled Sep. 28, 1912).

events.name: Emergency enrollment of ninety young men, Camp Cadwalader
events.description: Ninety young colored men enrolled and left for Harrisburg during the 1863 invasion, under Captain William H. Babe, depleting the ICY of teachers and scholars (recalled Jan. 3, 1914).

events.name: Frank Johnson's band plays before Queen Victoria
events.description: Frank Johnson's celebrated Philadelphia band toured England in 1838 and performed at Windsor Castle; Johnson was presented a gold-tipped bugle (recalled in Pencil Pusher Points, Apr. 1913).
